answers.text:
Perform the following steps on one of the affected Vostro 15 3510 devices and verify if the issue stops recurring.
Update the audio driver via Device Manager
In the search box on the taskbar, type device manager, then select it.
Expand Sound, video and game controllers.
Right‑click the internal audio device (e.g., Realtek/Intel audio), select Update driver.
Select Search automatically for drivers and complete the wizard.
Test audio in Edge/Chrome.
If the problem returns, clean‑reinstall the audio driver
Open Device Manager again and expand Sound, video and game controllers.
Right‑click the audio device and select Uninstall device.
Check Attempt to remove the driver for this device (or Delete the driver software for this device on Windows 10/11 variants), then select Uninstall.
Restart the PC (this will trigger automatic reinstallation of the audio driver).
Test audio in Edge and Chrome after reboot.
Try the generic Windows audio driver If the OEM driver keeps failing over time, switch to the generic driver that ships with Windows:
Open Device Manager → expand Sound, video and game controllers.
Right‑click the audio device → Update driver.
Select Browse my computer for drivers → Let me pick from a list of available drivers on my computer.
Select the generic audio driver listed there and complete the installation.
Restart if prompted and test audio in Edge/Chrome.
If audio broke after a Windows Update, roll back the driver
Open Device Manager → expand Sound, video and game controllers.
Right‑click the audio device → Properties.
Go to the Driver tab and select Roll Back Driver (if available).
Follow the prompts and confirm with Yes.
Test audio in Edge/Chrome.
If rollback is unavailable or ineffective, restore from a system restore point Windows creates restore points when installing updates. Restoring to a point where audio was stable can resolve driver regressions. Follow the "Restore from a system restore point" steps in Recovery options in Windows referenced in the documentation.
If, after these steps, audio still periodically disappears only in browsers while system sounds continue to work, keep the generic Windows audio driver in place and avoid reinstalling the OEM driver, as the OEM package may not be fully stable on Windows 11 for this model.

answers.text:
Follow these steps to troubleshoot saving to PDF from Microsoft Edge on Windows 11:
Test whether the issue is specific to the current page
Open a different website or online document in Edge.
Press Ctrl+P or select Settings and more (⋯) > Print.
In the Print dialog, choose Save as PDF and try to print.
If this works on other sites, the original page may have damaged graphics, fonts, or code. In that case:
Open the problematic site.
Select the Immersive Reader icon in the address bar (if available).
When the page opens in reading mode, press Ctrl+P and try Save as PDF again.
Check if the problem is Edge‑specific
Open another application such as Word.
Create a new blank document and type =rand(10) then press Enter to generate sample text.
Try printing that document to a real printer (not to PDF).
If printing fails from Word and other apps, the issue is likely with the printer driver, Windows, or connectivity; follow the Windows printer troubleshooting steps in the referenced article.
If printing works from all other apps but not from Edge, the problem is likely Edge or the way Edge interacts with the print system.
Retry printing and restart Edge
Close the Print dialog in Edge by selecting Cancel.
Open Print again (Ctrl+P) and select Save as PDF, then try to print.
If it still fails, close all Edge windows, reopen Edge, go back to the same page, and try Save as PDF again.
Verify other print targets
In the Edge Print dialog, try printing the same selection to a physical printer instead of Save as PDF.
If that also fails, use the steps above to test other sites/documents and other apps to isolate whether the problem is with Edge, the page, or the system print components.
If none of these steps resolve the issue and other apps print correctly, report the problem from Edge via Settings and more > Help and feedback > Send feedback, including the page URL, what was selected, and the exact steps taken before the failure.

Troubleshoot print issues in Microsoft Edge

title: I've had an issue saving files

question.text:
Routinely I download and save files the same way I've always done for years, but now for the past few weeks I've routinely noticed that a download will stop downloading or only save as a TMP file instead of the designated file type and the download will not complete nor will I be able to try and save anything else until all temp files area deleted and/or the whole computer is restarted
I never had this issue until recently and now it's returning time and time again, how do I prevent it from happening in the first place?
comments: Hi sonic, just wondering if you had the time to check out the suggestions in this thread? If there's any steps weren't clear enough, feel free to let me know!

I've done the suggestions but now I have to keep doing these and oftentimes that's not enough and I still need to restart the entire computer
This happens not just once a week but multiple times within a week
I'm sorry but these suggestions aren't getting to the core of the issue because the issue keeps repeating.

Hi, since the cause of the issue (as I stated in the original answer) is the download getting interrupted, have you tried with another user profile, local user, or different network (e.g. public wi-fi spot, your mobile data...) instead?
If you're signed into Edge, try browsing without signing in or using another account.
Try creating another local Windows user and use Edge for download there. If it works fine in another profile then something in your current one is corrupted, you can move to that one.
If it's Internet problem then unfortunately, there's nothing we can do from Windows-side.

For further clarification, it's definitely Edge because I can save files from Discord and Firefox just fine. Edge itself needs to have the entire computer restarted in order to fix the issue until it appears again
Trying another account with Edge might not fix the issue though but I'm willing to give it a go I guess

answers.text:
Hi sonic,
I’m sorry to hear that the downloads that used to work reliably now stall or leave only .TMP files and block further saves until a restart. Normally, Edge saves downloads as temporary files first, then renames them when completes. If something interferes mid‑download, the .TMP file never gets finalized. If the Q&A Assist's suggestions couldn't help, here’s something else you can check out:
1 - Clear Edge download cache only (not browsing history)
Open Edge > Press Ctrl + Shift + Del to open Clear browsing data
Checked Cached images and files only > Clear now > Restart Edge.
2 - Change Edge’s default download location
Open Edge > Settings > Downloads > Location > click Change
Make a new folder for testing, e.g. D:\Downloads_Test
Disable Ask where to save each file before downloading
If downloads work, the original has a permission/sync issue.
3 - Check Windows Security folder protection
Open Windows Security > go to Virus & threat protection
Ransomware protection > If Controlled folder access is enabled:
Turn it off temporarily for testing
Or add msedge.exe to the allow list
4 - Temporarily pause OneDrive sync
If OneDrive is running, click the logo in the taskbar tray
Click the Settings > choose to pause OneDrive sync
Test downloads again if it’s working. If yes, it's a sync issue.
5 - Test with a new Windows user profile
Open Settings > Accounts > add a new local account
Sign into this one and test the downloads there
If they work, the original profile is broken.
Let me know if any of the suggestions above works, or it’s still blocked. We will go through more nuclear options depending on the results of the solutions above.

Trying another account with Edge might not fix the issue though but I'm willing to give it a go I guess

answers.text:
The behavior matches known issues where downloads fail or temporary/cache files are deleted or blocked before the final file is created. Several system components can cause this: Internet/Edge security settings, Attachment Manager, antivirus integration, or underlying Windows/IE download bugs.
To reduce or prevent this from happening:
Verify that file downloads are allowed
Open the Internet Properties dialog (press Windows key, type inetcpl.cpl, press Enter).
Go to the Security tab, select the Internet zone (globe icon), and choose Custom level.
In Security Settings:
Scroll to Downloads → File download and set it to Enable.
Scroll to Miscellaneous → Launching applications and unsafe files and set it to Prompt (recommended).
Select OK to apply.
This ensures downloads are not being blocked by security zone configuration.
Check for antivirus/Attachment Manager interference
If messages like Virus scan failed or Virus detected appear, the download is typically being blocked by antivirus or Attachment Manager, not by Windows itself.
If the download source is trusted, temporarily disable virus scanning only for testing, then re-enable it immediately after confirming behavior.
Attachment Manager behavior is controlled under the registry key:
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Attachments
Misconfiguration here can cause downloads to be blocked or temp files to be removed before completion. Adjustments should be done carefully because incorrect registry edits can expose the system to attacks.
Keep Windows and browser components fully updated
Several known download issues (including cache/temp file handling and failures when saving) have been fixed by cumulative security updates for Internet Explorer and related components. Even though Edge is used, the underlying Windows networking and download stack shares components with IE.
Install the latest cumulative security updates for Windows and browser components via Windows Update (Settings → Windows Update → Check for updates). This includes fixes where cache files were deleted before being copied to the final temp file, causing intermittent download failures.
Clear temporary Internet/cache files when the issue appears
When downloads start failing and only .tmp files appear, clearing the browser cache and temporary Internet files can release locked handles and allow new downloads to complete.
If clearing cache does not help, a full restart (as already done) resets the download stack and usually restores normal behavior temporarily.
If the problem keeps recurring even after confirming download settings, checking antivirus behavior, and applying all Windows updates, the next step is to look for a specific update or hotfix for the download component in use and ensure it is installed, as Microsoft has confirmed and fixed multiple download-related bugs in past updates.
References:
Information about the Attachment Manager in Microsoft Windows
File downloading intermittently deletes cache file before it copies to temp file in Internet Explorer 11

title: Open links outside Edge in a new window

question.text:
Greetings.
I'm not sure if this is something related to Windows or Edge, but since a couple weeks the behaviour has changed regarding opening links from apps outside Edge.
I use several virtual desktops and I usuallly I leave Edge windows opened in these desktops. Normally, when I'm in a desktop that doesn't have an Edge window opened, whenever I click on a weblink in any app (Outlook, Teams, etc.) a new Edge window would open in that desktop. But since a couple of weeks, links now open in a different desktop that has an Edge window opened, so I find myself jumping from desktop to desktop without wanting to.
I don't know if anyone is familiar with this issue. Hopefully there's a fix.
Thank you in advance,
comments:
Hello Juan,
Just check in to see how things are going. Let me know if you need anything.

answers.text:
The behavior change is caused by a new Microsoft 365/Edge integration where links from Outlook and Teams open in Microsoft Edge with a side-by-side view of the email/chat and the web page. This integration can change how and where Edge windows are opened when links are clicked from those apps.
To restore more traditional behavior (and reduce unwanted desktop switching), configure which browser opens links from Outlook and Teams, or allow users to choose:
If managed by policy (IT/admin scenario)
Use the Choose Which Browser Opens Web Links policy.
Set it to System default browser if links should follow the Windows default browser, instead of forcing Edge.
This policy applies to Outlook for Windows and Teams for Windows and controls which browser opens web links from those apps.
If users manage their own settings
Leave the Choose Which Browser Opens Web Links policy Not configured or Disabled.
Then, in each app, users can choose how links open:
New Outlook for Windows: Settings > General > Files and links > Open hyperlinks from Outlook in.
Classic Outlook for Windows: File > Options > Advanced > File and browser preferences.
Teams: Settings > Files and links > Link open preference.
On first use, a banner in Edge may appear; selecting Manage my settings lets users revert to their system default browser instead of Edge.
Adjusting these settings or policy will stop Outlook/Teams from forcing links into an existing Edge integration window and should reduce the cross-desktop jumping when opening links from those apps.
References:
Web links from Outlook emails and Teams chats open in Microsoft Edge
Web links from Outlook and Teams open in Microsoft Edge in side-by-side view

Hello Juan,
Thanks for reaching out. A similar case was discussed here: Microsoft Edge switches Virtual Desktops (pulls focus) when it is open in multiple desktops
From my understanding, Edge runs as a single-instance app by default, so when you click a link from another app, Windows may route it to an existing Edge window on a different desktop instead of opening a new one.
You can try a few workarounds:
Isolate Edge windows per desktop — Open Task View (Win + Tab), right-click the Edge window, and uncheck "Show windows from this app on all desktops." This keeps Edge windows tied to the desktop they were opened on.
Use separate Edge profiles per desktop — Click your profile icon in Edge and select "Add profile." Use a different profile on each virtual desktop. Edge treats each profile as a separate process, which reduces cross-desktop switching.
Check Multitasking settings — Go to Settings > System > Multitasking > Desktops > "Show open windows" and set it to "Only on the desktop I'm using."
Also, submitting feedback directly in Edge (Alt + Shift + I) helps the Edge team track this behavior.

answers.text:
The error indicates that required user session context (cookies, form fields, or headers) is not being preserved between requests on the iPhone. This typically happens when the browser or embedded web view used for sign-in does not allow cookies or does not share them correctly.
For iOS sign-in flows that rely on Microsoft identity (including Microsoft 365 and Entra ID–backed apps), the sign-in experience depends on cookies being stored and reused to maintain a single user flow. If cookies are blocked, cleared, or isolated between requests, the service treats each request as a new session and fails with an error like AADSTS165000.
To address this on iOS:
Ensure that the browser or web view used for sign-in allows cookies and does not block them for Microsoft domains.
Avoid custom or embedded browsers that do not support normal cookie sharing for authentication flows.
If an app uses MSAL on iOS with a custom browser configuration (for example, SFSafariViewController, SFAuthenticationSession, ASWebAuthenticationSession, or WKWebView), configure it so that cookies can be shared and preserved across the entire sign-in flow, or revert to the default Safari system browser behavior, which supports SSO state sharing and proper token/cookie handling.
These steps ensure that the required user context values (cookies and related state) are maintained across the complete sign-in flow, preventing repeated failures with missing session context.
References:
Troubleshoot sign-in prompt issues in iOS with MSAL SDK

Hi aReply4u0084,
Thanks again for your detailed feedback. Based on your observations, it seems the pop-ups began appearing after Google introduced the AI icon in the search bar. This icon may be triggering promotional overlays, similar to the horizontal ads Google used in the past to promote Chrome.
If you're interested in keeping the AI features active, but want to stop the pop-ups, here are a few options you can try:
Registry Tweak to Disable Promotional Content
This tweak disables all promotional suggestions in Edge, including Google’s Chrome ads and sign-in prompts.
Press Win + R, type regedit, and press Enter.
Navigate to:
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Edge
Right-click the Edge folder > New > DWORD (32-bit) Value.
Name it: ShowRecommendationsEnabled
Set the value to 0.
Restart Edge.
*Disclaimer: Generally, modifying registry subkeys or work groups is intended for advanced users, administrators, and IT Professionals. It can help fix some problems, however, serious problems might occur if you modify the registry incorrectly. Therefore, make sure that you follow these steps carefully. For further protection, back up the registry before you modify it. Then, you can restore the registry if a problem occurs. For more information about how to back up and restore the registry, click How to back up and restore the registry in Windows - Microsoft Support to view the article. 
Strict Tracking Prevention
Google’s popups may be triggered by tracking cookies.
Go to: edge://settings/privacy
Set Tracking prevention to Strict
Clear cookies and site data for google.com
Consider Changing Your Homepage
If your Edge homepage or new tab page is set to google.com, please consider switching to a different page may help avoid triggering these popups:
about:blank (a completely empty page)
edge://newtab (Edge’s default new tab experience)
Or a custom homepage that doesn’t load Google directly

answers.text:
Hi aReply4u0084,
Thank you for contacting Microsoft Q&A. I understand how disruptive it can be to encounter recurring sign-in prompts, particularly when using Microsoft Edge as your preferred browser. I appreciate you bringing this to our attention and sharing the details of your experience.
The popup you are seeing in the lower-right corner of the Edge homepage is a prompt from Google, encouraging users to sign in to their Google account. This behavior is typically triggered when Google is set as the default search engine and the user is not currently signed in. While this may be intended to enhance cross-platform synchronization, it can understandably feel intrusive, especially when using a browser other than Chrome.
To help you resolve this issue, I recommend these following steps along with disabling extensions on Edge as per above suggestion:
Step 1: Disable Google Account Sign-In Prompts
This method works across all browsers and devices:
Visit Google My Account.
Navigate to Security > Your Connections to Third-Party Apps & Services.
Click See All Connections.
Tap the gear icon in the top-right corner.
Toggle off Google Account Sign-In Prompts.
You may need to repeat this process for each Google account you are signed into. Once disabled, this setting should prevent the prompt from appearing across all browsers.
*Disclaimer: Microsoft provides no assurances and/or warranties, implied or otherwise, and is not responsible for the information you receive from the third-party linked sites or any support related to technology. 
Step 2: Block Popups and disable notifications from Google in Microsoft Edge
Open Edge and go to Settings > Privacy, search, and services > Site permissions > All permissions > Pop-ups and redirects.
Ensure Block (recommended) is enabled.
Add google.com to the Not allowed to send pop-ups or use redirects list to prevent further prompts.
Also in Privacy, search, and services > Site permissions > All permissions > Notifications > Customized behaviors
Scroll to Allow and locate google.com.
Click the three dots next to it and select Block.
This prevents Google from sending notification-based prompts, including sign-in suggestions.
Step 3: Clear Cookies and Cache
Clearing stored browsing data may help remove session information that triggers the popup:
Go to Settings > Privacy, search, and services.
Under Clear browsing data, select Cookies and other site data and Cached images and files.
Click Clear now.
Step 4: Use a Different User Profile in Edge
Creating a new profile can help determine whether the issue is tied to your current profile’s settings or extensions:
Click your profile icon in the top-right corner of Edge.
Select Add profile and choose Start without signing in.
Set up the new profile and test whether the popup still appears.
Please keep me posted on how it goes, and feel free to reach out if you need any further assistance. I'm here to help.

answers.text:
In Microsoft Edge on Windows 11, hidden or older bookmark-related data may still be available even if it looks like it has disappeared, depending on how it was stored and synced.
Check if favorites are just hidden by the Favorites bar setting
Select Settings and more (three dots in the top-right) > Settings.
Select Appearance.
Under Other appearance settings, select Toolbar, then locate Favorites bar.
Choose Always or Only on new tabs instead of Never to make sure the favorites bar is visible.
Verify that the correct Edge profile is in use and syncing
If a new profile was created (for example, “Profile 2”), favorites may be associated with a different profile.
In the top-right of Edge, select the profile icon and switch back to the original profile.
Ensure the profile is signed in and sync is turned on so favorites can be restored from the cloud.
Confirm that reading list and “tabs set aside” items are now in Favorites
Older Edge features like Reading list and Tabs you set aside are no longer separate; their items are moved into Favorites.
Select Settings and more > Favorites > Other favorites.
Look for folders such as Reading list saves and Tabs set aside to see if the missing items are stored there.
Use Windows backup and sync to restore Edge preferences
If Windows backup and Edge sync were enabled previously, signing into Edge with the same Microsoft account and turning on sync can restore favorites.
Open Settings > Accounts > Windows backup and ensure preferences are being remembered.
In Edge, sign in and enable sync so favorites and other browser data are pulled back from the cloud.
If favorites were deleted locally but sync was active, they may already be removed from the cloud as well; in that case, recovery options are limited beyond checking other profiles or devices where Edge is signed in.
References:
Hide your favorites bar in Microsoft Edge
Find your reading list, web notes, and tabs in Microsoft Edge
Back up and restore with Windows Backup

Font Settings in Applications: If you are using specific applications like Microsoft Edge or others, check their individual settings for font size adjustments. For example, in Microsoft Edge, you can adjust the font size in the settings under Appearance.
Reset Font Settings: If you have customized font settings, you might want to reset them to default. In applications like Visual Studio, you can go to Tools > Options > Environment > Fonts and Colors and select Use Defaults.
Accessibility Options: Ensure that any accessibility features that may affect font size are configured correctly. You can find these settings in the Ease of Access section of the Windows settings.
If these steps do not resolve the issue, consider checking for any recent updates or changes to your system that might have affected font settings.

In some situations, pinned tabs may not reappear if the browser session was not fully restored or if multiple Edge windows were closed in a specific order. You can try the following checking steps and workarounds:
Open Microsoft Edge > Settings
Go to Start, home, and new tabs
Under When Edge starts, select Open tabs from the previous session

answers.text:
Pinned tabs in Microsoft Edge are part of the current browsing session, not a separate, backed‑up list like Favorites or Reading list. When a session is lost (for example due to a crash, profile change, sign‑out, or certain settings), pinned tabs can disappear and they will not show up separately in History. Only the sites that were actually visited appear in History, and there is no special “pinned tabs” history view to restore them as a group.
To reduce the risk of losing important pinned tabs in the future and improve recovery options:
Save important pinned sites as Favorites
Open each site that needs to be kept.
Select Settings and more (… ) > Add to favorites.
Optionally create a folder (for example, “Pinned tabs”) so they can be reopened together.
This works because Edge keeps Favorites even when sessions are lost, and older features such as Reading list and “Tabs you set aside” were also migrated into Favorites.
Configure Edge to reopen previous tabs on startup
Go to Settings and more (… ) > Settings > Start, home, and new tab page.
Select Open tabs from the previous session.
With this enabled, Edge will reopen the last session’s tabs (including pinned ones) on startup, as long as the session data has not been cleared.
Be aware of limits to session restore
If browsing data is configured to be cleared on close, the content of the session can be removed even when “Open tabs from the previous session” is selected; only the URLs needed for the next startup are kept.
Tabs from InPrivate or Guest mode are never restored on startup.
If Edge is not reliably restoring tabs
If tabs (including pinned ones) frequently fail to restore even with the startup setting enabled, repair Edge:
Close Edge for all users.
Open Start > Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
Select Microsoft Edge > Modify.
Approve the prompt and choose Repair.
This keeps browser data and settings but can fix issues that prevent normal session behavior.
Because pinned tabs are session‑based, once they are gone and not present in the current session or visible in History, Edge does not provide a built‑in way to reconstruct that exact pinned set. Using Favorites (and folders) plus the “Open tabs from the previous session” startup option is the supported way to keep important sites from being lost.
References:
User data and privacy in Microsoft Edge
What to do if Microsoft Edge isn't working
Find your reading list, web notes, and tabs in Microsoft Edge

title: Edge Doesn't Block ads on Yahoo! on same Devices

question.text:
MS Edge finally resolved re-blocking ads on Yahoo! but it only does on my HP laptop but not on my Surface Go 2. Both are running Windows 11 except the HP is running Home and the Surface Go 2 is Pro. MS Edge is the exact same build 147.0.3912.72 (Official build) (64-bit).
Things I've tried:
MS Edge reinstall-repair
Delete cookies et all
Install Edge Beta
Delete User Data folder
Delete Microsoft.MicrosoftEdge folder
I have tried various ad blockers but Edge blocks fine on my HP without them.
What could be making the HP work but not the Surface GO 2?
comments: Everything matched except; 1. Surface Go 2, Use secure DNS was disabled, 2. HP laptop, edge://components all displayed Status - Update error, and 3. HP shows over 1,200 blocked trackers, Surface Go 2 shows 400 something.

Hi Don ‪B
What you’re seeing actually points to privacy/config behavior differences.
From your results:
HP: ~1200 blocked trackers + Secure DNS likely active → fewer Yahoo ads
Surface: ~400 trackers + Secure DNS OFF → more trackers allowed → ads show
This aligns with how Edge works, ads on Yahoo depend heavily on tracker blocking, not a dedicated ad blocker.
What to try on your Surface:
Enable Use secure DNS (match HP exactly, including provider)
Set Tracking prevention to Strict (test impact)
Check edge://components → force update Trust Protection Lists
Restart Edge after changes
If behavior changes after these, it confirms it’s tracker classification + DNS behavior, not OS edition or Edge build.

I forgot to mention that I did turn on Secure DNS. And I always have Tracking Protection on Strict. The edge://components that were not updating were on the HP, not the Surface Go 2. My HP doesn't exhibit fewer ads. It literally has no ads is what I am used to.
My Surface Go 2 used to block all ads. Also, this only happens on Yahoo.com. I've long since cleared all caches and Yahoo cookies but to no avail.

Hi Don ‪B
After all the troubleshooting, this confirms it, it has came down to device-based behavior. Edge tracking prevention relies on locally stored tracker classification lists (Trust Protection Lists), and these are maintained per device, so two devices can block very different sets of trackers even with identical settings and versions.
There’s no reliable way to force them to behave identically, if you need consistent ad blocking (especially on sites like Yahoo), you’ll need a dedicated content blocker rather than relying on Edge’s built-in tracking prevention.
I know this isn't the answer you're looking for, but it is designed that way and there's no further fix we can try.
You can still try contacting Microsoft Support directly to explore their expertise on this at https://support.microsoft.com/contactus
Have a great day!

I hear you but for years they used to be identical. Then for some reason, Yahoo! ads were not blocked at all on any device.
I sent feedback and after a while later, an update blocked Yahoo! ads but not consistently on my Surface Go 2. If I toggle Strict tracking protection off and then on, Yahoo! ads are blocked but only temporarily on the Surface Go 2.
Ads are blocked consistently on all other sites except Yahoo!.

answers.text:
Hi Don ‪B
If Edge blocks Yahoo ads on one PC but not the other with the same Edge build, the cause is almost always device‑level settings or policies, not Edge itself.
A few things you can check on the Surface Go 2 (Windows 11 Pro):
Tracking prevention level (edge://settings/privacy) - it must match exactly (Balanced or Strict) and have no Yahoo exceptions.
Edge policies (edge://policy) - Pro can enforce Local Group Policy/MDM settings that Home cannot. If anything appears here, Edge behavior will differ.
Personalized advertising & experiences - if enabled on the Surface but disabled on the HP, Yahoo ads will reappear.
Secure DNS (DoH) settings - differences can affect tracker blocking.
Trust Protection Lists (edge://components) - different versions/refresh states can temporarily change tracker blocking.
This explains why the HP (Home) works but the Surface (Pro) doesn’t, even with identical Edge versions.

title: MS Edge windows closes shortly after starting, repair function/sfc scannow not helping

question.text:
Hi there
MS edge has been behaving strangely of late on my Lenovo notebook, with Win 11 Pro, ALL available Windows Updates installed, with AMD Ryzen 7 5800U, 16GB RAM, all Lenovo drivers/software up-to-date. I have used Windscribe VPN add-on for years with no issue. It suddenly stopped working with some error message and when I tried to re-install it, the process always failed. Today, suddenly, when an application, ArcGIS failed to open a link in Edge, I tried opening it manually and noticed a blank window briefly appears and then disappears even before it gets fully loaded.
I tried using the Apps => repair function, it did not help. Both sfc /scannow & DISM restore-health find no violation and even after restart, no change.
So what can I do to get MS Edge working again?
I read one can completely uninstall it, though it was mentioned that could be problematic, and re-install it, but have not tried that yet.
I would very much appreciate your help, with clear instructions.
Kind regards

I had already tried out some of the solutions suggested in the AI response, then did the rest, including trying to install Edge over the existing faulty one. All did not solve the problem.
I then went on to try an In Place Installation to try and repair Windows with a freshly downloaded .iso file, but frustratingly, the set-up fails trying to validate the key of the Win 11 Pro (which I bought online) and successfully upgraded from Win 11 Home a couple of months ago ...
and activated with no problem. So I am now stuck with a completely different unconnected issue.

Strangely, the key that I got from the vendor looks different from the one I see when I use some command (Get-WmiObject -query 'select * from SoftwareLicensingService').OA3xOriginalProductKey ... to retrieve it. Quite confusing for an amateur.
Will appreciate your assistance, apparently for both issues and the last question.
Furthermore, under Apps, the Modify option for Edge is deactivated, with only Uninstall. Is it safe to uninstall & re-install from a fresh downloaded file?
Regards

answers.text:
Based on the described symptoms (Edge window opens briefly then closes, add-ons failing to install, sfc/DISM showing no issues), use the following Edge‑specific troubleshooting steps before attempting a full uninstall.
Close all Edge processes and other apps
Make sure no Edge windows are open.
Close other apps, especially those that might use embedded Edge/WebView2 (for example, Teams, new Outlook, widgets, or ArcGIS components) so no Edge files are locked.
Check that Edge can update and is on a supported OS
Confirm Windows 11 is fully updated (already done) and that the device meets Edge OS requirements.
Try to start Edge once more and, if it stays open long enough, go to:
Settings and more (… ) > Settings > About Microsoft Edge and let it check for and apply any Edge updates.
General Edge crash troubleshooting When Edge opens then closes or fails to load pages:
Free up memory:
Close all other browser tabs, apps, and pause any downloads.
Restart the device and try Edge again.
Scan the device for malware using Windows Security (Windows 11 uses the same Windows Security experience as Windows 10 referenced in the article). Follow the “Run a scan manually” steps in Windows Security and remove any detected threats.
If third‑party security software (for example, Windscribe’s desktop client, other AV/VPN) is installed, ensure it is fully updated. Out‑of‑date security software can interfere with Edge processes and extensions.
Repair Microsoft Edge from Apps settings Since the standard “Repair” from Apps was already tried, repeat it ensuring all prerequisites are met:
Close Edge for all user accounts on the device.
Confirm the signed‑in account has local administrator rights.
Go to Start > Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
Select Microsoft Edge > Modify.
Approve the UAC prompt.
Ensure the device is online and choose Repair. This process reinstalls Edge components while preserving browser data and settings.
Run the Program Compatibility Troubleshooter for Edge If Edge still closes immediately, run the Windows Program Compatibility Troubleshooter against Edge:
Select Start > Settings > Update & Security.
Select Troubleshoot > Additional troubleshooters.
Under Find and fix other problems, select Program Compatibility Troubleshooter.
Select Run the troubleshooter and follow the prompts, choosing Microsoft Edge when asked which program to troubleshoot.
Clear Edge update cache Corrupted update cache can cause instability and failed extension installs.
Navigate to C:\Users\<YourUserName>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Edge\Update\ and delete the contents of the Update folder.
Restart the device and try launching Edge again.
Reinstall Edge on top of the existing installation If Edge still will not stay open, reinstall it over the current installation (this is safer than trying to fully remove the built‑in copy):
Open another working browser (for example, Firefox, which is already in use).
Go to the official Edge download page and download the latest installer.
After download, close all apps that might use Edge/WebView2.
Right‑click the Edge installer and select Run as administrator.
Complete setup; this will reinstall/repair Edge in place.
If reinstall still fails or Edge will not start
Verify there is at least 1–2 GB of free space on the system drive.
Restart the device again to clear any pending updates and locked files, then retry the in‑place Edge reinstall.
If the problem persists after all steps above, collect details (any error codes, event log entries) and contact Microsoft Support, as recommended in similar cases where in‑place repair and reinstall did not resolve Edge startup failures.
These steps address common causes of Edge crashing on startup, failed extension installs, and update corruption without attempting unsupported full removal of Edge from Windows.

What to do if Microsoft Edge isn't working
Troubleshooting tips for downloading, installing, and updating Microsoft Edge
Install, update, or roll back failures for Edge and Edge WebView2

Check sound devices and enhancements
Right-click the speaker icon in the taskbar → Sound settings.
Under Output, select the active output device (Speakers / Surface Audio).
Scroll to Audio enhancements and turn them Off to rule out enhancement-related tones.
Under Input, confirm the correct microphone is selected and working; misconfigured input can sometimes trigger system sounds.
Adjust microphone settings (if chime is related to mic activity)
Open Sound settings: Open Sound settings
Under Input > Choose a device for speaking or recording, select the correct mic.
Ensure "Allow apps and Windows to use this device for audio" is enabled.
Adjust Input volume so it is not constantly peaking, and turn off any Audio enhancements.
Use Start test to verify the mic is behaving normally.
Reinstall audio driver (even if already updated)
Press Win + X → Device Manager.
Expand Sound, video and game controllers.
Right-click Realtek High Definition Audio or Surface Audio → Uninstall device.
Check Delete the driver software if available → Uninstall.
Restart the Surface Laptop 5.
After restart, go to Windows Update → Check for updates and install all updates, including Optional updates, so the correct Surface audio driver and firmware are reinstalled.
Verify Windows audio services
Press Win + R, type services.msc, press Enter.
Find Windows Audio and Windows Audio Endpoint Builder.
For each service:
Ensure Startup type is Automatic.
Select Restart.
Update Surface firmware and drivers via Surface app
Open the Surface app: Open the Surface app.
Expand Help & support and check update status.
Select Check for updates to open Windows Update and install all available updates.
If the Surface app is not installed, install it from Microsoft Store, then repeat the steps above.
If the chime persists, consider a reset path
If all audio, driver, and service checks are clean and the chime continues, use the Surface recovery options:
First try a standard restart.
If needed, perform a reset/restore of the Surface from Windows recovery options, after backing up important data.
If the chime is tied to a specific action (plugging/unplugging power, USB, Bluetooth, or notifications), repeat the above steps while watching Device Manager and Sound settings to see which device or input is repeatedly connecting/disconnecting.

answers.text:
Follow these steps focused on Edge, since Microsoft Print to PDF works in other apps and the crash is specific to Edge:
Close other tabs, extensions, and apps
Edge may be running out of memory when invoking Print to PDF.
Close all tabs except the one being printed.
Close other running apps and pause any downloads.
Disable or uninstall unnecessary extensions.
Try printing to PDF again.
Update Microsoft Edge
A bug in the current Edge build can cause crashes during printing.
In Edge, select Settings and more (⋯) > Settings > About Microsoft Edge.
Allow Edge to check for and install updates.
Restart Edge and test Microsoft Print to PDF again.
Clear Edge cache and browsing data
Corrupted cached data can cause page or print rendering issues.
In Edge, select Settings and more > History > Open history page.
Select Delete browsing data.
Set Time range to All time.
Select at least Browsing history and Cached images and files, then choose Clear now.
Retry printing to PDF.
Repair Microsoft Edge
If Edge components related to printing are corrupted, repairing Edge can fix them without removing data.
Close Edge for all users on the device.
Go to Start > Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
Select Microsoft Edge > Modify.
When prompted, choose Yes, ensure the device is online, then select Repair.
After repair completes, open Edge and test Microsoft Print to PDF.
Restart the device and re‑test
A running process or driver conflict can cause Edge to crash when invoking the print pipeline.
Restart Windows.
Open Edge and immediately test printing to PDF on a simple page (for example, a blank or text-only page) before opening many tabs.
Check security software
Third‑party security tools can interfere with Edge’s print or PDF pipeline.
Open any third‑party security software (for example, Norton, McAfee).
Ensure it is fully updated.
If it has web or browser protection modules, temporarily disable them and test printing to PDF in Edge.
If the crash stops, configure the security software to trust Edge.
Isolate content or site issues
Since printing works in other apps, also test whether the crash happens on all sites or only specific ones:
Try printing a different website or a local HTML file in Edge.
If only certain sites cause the crash, capture that detail and use Settings and more > Help and feedback > Send feedback in Edge to report it.
If the crash persists after these steps, collect crash information (for example, via edge://crashes if crash reporting is enabled) and provide it when contacting support, as crash diagnostic data is used to identify and fix Edge issues.

Troubleshoot print issues in Microsoft Edge
What to do if Microsoft Edge isn't working
User data and privacy in Microsoft Edge
Recent Microsoft Edge update causing printer issues - Microsoft Q&A

title: Microsoft Edge 145, kiosk mode downloads

question.text: I have been using edge kiosk with public browsing but when I updated to 145 I lost the ability to view downloads. I am able to get to edge://downloads which is what it was in the past but now it looks like edge is using Edge://downloads-hub which kiosk mode blocks. Edge://downloads still works but I use read only address bar and the downloads button on the edge menu no longer directs to downloads
comments: I've had the same issue, and after a lot of troubleshooting I found that going to the kiosk's localappdata\Microsoft and renaming the Edge folder to recreate the Edge profile fixes it for me

answers.text:
In Microsoft Edge kiosk public-browsing mode, internal URLs are locked down and only edge://downloads and edge://print are allowed. All other internal Edge URLs, including the newer downloads hub (edge://downloads-hub), are blocked by design.
In current kiosk behavior, this means:
Access to edge://downloads is still the supported and allowed way to view downloads.
If the UI or redirects in version 145 attempt to use edge://downloads-hub, kiosk mode will block that page and there is no kiosk policy to override this.
To work around this within kiosk constraints:
Ensure the kiosk session is explicitly navigating to edge://downloads (for example via a direct link or allowed navigation) rather than relying on any built‑in button that may target edge://downloads-hub.
Confirm that kiosk is configured as Public browsing and that no additional URL blocking policies (such as URLBlocklist) are preventing edge://downloads.
If the browser itself is internally redirecting edge://downloads to edge://downloads-hub in version 145, that conflicts with the documented kiosk rule that only edge://downloads and edge://print are allowed. In that case, this is a product regression/behavior change and needs to be raised to Microsoft Edge support or via enterprise support channels, as there is no kiosk policy or flag in the documented configuration to re‑allow edge://downloads-hub.
References:
Configure Microsoft Edge kiosk mode
Plan your kiosk mode transition
Archived release notes for Microsoft Edge Stable Channel

title: How to erase ALL passwords in Edge?

question.text:
I want to erase all saved passwords in Edge.
I’ve tried everything, but after deleting them one by one, they keep coming back.
I’ve also tried resetting the Edge settings, unsyncing the passwords tab, clearing browsing data, and more, but nothing has worked.
If anyone has any ideas, I would greatly appreciate it."

I have also tried re-syncing with Microsoft Server as well as signing out and deleting all history, as suggested in this post. Nothing is working and this is completely frustrating. I am being forced to delete passwords one by one
Same, it is easy to import and outport, but not to delete.
Your instructions provided did not work.

answers.text:

Please remove the silly AI generated content, and get a real person to look at it.
I am using Version 147.0.3912.60 (Official build) (64-bit) if that matters.
comments: Same, it is easy to import and outport, but not to delete.

answers.text:
Hi,
It does seem like there’s no option for Passwords when clearing browsing data, and it’s unclear whether that’s intentional or a bug. If your passwords keep reappearing, it’s likely because your synced data from the server is restoring them, including your saved passwords. In this case, you can try resetting your sync data from the server in Settings > Profiles > Sync > Reset sync data from Microsoft servers. I can't remember if this will affect Workspaces, but you can use this extension to export your Favorites per workspace as backup in case it got affected.
If that doesn't work, you can go to Settings > Profiles > Sync, then sign out and choose the option to clear favorites, history, and passwords before proceeding. After that, you can sign back into your account. This method works if you’re okay with wiping everything and starting fresh.
comments: Your instructions provided did not work.

answers.text: IN the section "Clear browsing data", there is no "PASSWORD" option to Check, only "all data from the previous version of Microsoft Edge!

To erase all saved passwords in Microsoft Edge and prevent them from reappearing, clear them from both the local browser data and from any synced profile data, then turn off saving going forward.
Delete all saved passwords from Edge
Open Edge.
Select Settings and more (⋯) > Settings.
Go to Privacy, search, and services.
Under Clear browsing data, select Choose what to clear next to Clear browsing data now.
Set Time range to All time.
Check Passwords (scroll down if needed).
Select Clear now.
This removes all passwords stored on the device (and, if sync is on, across synced devices).
Make sure password saving is disabled so new ones are not recreated
In Edge, go to Settings and more (⋯) > Settings.
Select Profiles > Passwords.
Turn Offer to save passwords to Off.
Turning this off stops Edge from offering to save passwords again. Previously saved passwords are already removed by step 1.
If passwords still reappear, repeat on every device using the same profile Because passwords can sync across devices, repeat step 1 on each device where the same Edge profile is signed in and syncing. Ensure sync is turned off for passwords while doing this, then turn it back on only after all devices have been cleared.

Microsoft Edge, browsing data, and privacy

Save or forget passwords in Microsoft Edge

answers.text:
To make your taskbar larger in Windows 11, you can follow these steps:
Right-click on the Taskbar: This will bring up a context menu.
Select Taskbar Settings: This will open the Taskbar settings page.
Adjust the Size: Look for an option related to taskbar size. In Windows 11, the taskbar size is generally fixed, but you can adjust the scaling settings in the display settings to indirectly affect the taskbar size.
Change Display Scaling: Go to Settings > System > Display. Under Scale and layout, you can change the scaling percentage. Increasing this percentage will make everything on your screen, including the taskbar, appear larger.
If you are unable to find the dropdown page or settings, ensure that your Windows 11 is updated to the latest version, as settings can sometimes change with updates.

answers.text:
To turn off the news feed in Microsoft Edge, use the page layout/custom settings rather than the “new look” toggle.
On desktop Edge (new tab / Start page):
Open a new tab.
Select the settings cog in the upper‑right of the page.
Choose Custom.
Under content options, select Content off (this fully turns off the feed).
• Alternatively, choose Focused to hide the feed until scrolling.
On Edge mobile (Android/iOS):
Open Edge and a new tab.
Tap Page layout at the top right.
Tap Custom.
Turn Show feeds off.
If these options are missing, ensure the latest Edge version is installed, as older builds expose different layout/feed controls.
